name: Bhaskara
dates_notes: Roughly contemporary with Śaṅkara; dated from internal references and citation chronology.

lineage_polemical.note: Bhāskara explicitly rejects Śaṅkara's māyā as a smuggled-in Buddhist concept; the polemic runs both directions through later commentary.

core_thesis: Bhāskara's *aupādhika-bhedābheda* holds that *bheda* (difference) and *abheda* (non-difference) are equally real features of the Brahman–world relation, but that *bheda* arises through *upādhi* — the limiting adjuncts of body, mind, and the conditions of empirical existence — while *abheda* is the underlying ontological truth. Brahman genuinely transforms (*pariṇāma*) into the manifest world; the world is therefore real, not appearance. The individual *jīva* is non-different from Brahman in essence yet really different so long as adjuncts persist; liberation is the dropping of *upādhi*, not the discovery that difference was illusory. Bhāskara explicitly rejects Śaṅkara's *māyā-vāda* as a *pracchanna-bauddha* (covertly Buddhist) doctrine smuggled into Vedānta — the charge appears in his Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya itself. He defends *jagat-satyatva* (the reality of the world) and *karma-jñāna-samuccaya* (the joint efficacy of action and knowledge for liberation) against the Advaitic insistence that knowledge alone, by exposing the world as *mithyā*, suffices.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sutra-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the earliest extant Bhedābhedin commentary on the Brahma-Sūtra.
engaged_works.summary: Bhāskara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* (often called the *Bhāskarīya-Bhāṣya*) is the only major extant early Bhedābheda commentary on the *Brahma-Sūtras* and a direct rival to Śaṅkara's, possibly composed in dialogue with it. Its philosophical work is twofold: it constructs a positive *aupādhika-bhedābheda* reading of the *sūtras*, on which Brahman undergoes real transformation (*pariṇāma*) into a real world, with *bheda* arising through *upādhi*; and it mounts a sustained polemic against *māyā-vāda*, which Bhāskara famously diagnoses as a *pracchanna-bauddha* (covertly Buddhist) intrusion into Vedānta. Doctrines defended include *jagat-satyatva*, the reality and irreducibility of plural *jīvas* under embodied conditions, and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* (knowledge and ritual action as jointly efficacious for liberation). The bhāṣya thereby preserves an alternative early-medieval Vedānta in which neither Śaṅkara's *vivarta* nor Rāmānuja's later *viśiṣṭādvaita* governs the reading of *śruti*.

engaged_works.title: Gita-Bhashya fragments
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Surviving in fragmentary form; ascription accepted but the text is incomplete.
engaged_works.summary: A *Gītā-Bhāṣya* is ascribed to Bhāskara but survives only in fragmentary citations preserved by later commentators (notably Madhusūdana and several Advaita and Bhedābheda doxographies). The fragments, where doctrinally legible, extend the *aupādhika-bhedābheda* programme of the *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*: *karma* and *jñāna* are read as jointly efficacious rather than as successive stages collapsing into pure *jñāna*; the *jīva* is taken as really, not illusorily, distinct from Brahman under the conditions of embodiment; and the *Gītā*'s theistic vocabulary is treated as straightforward rather than as an interim concession to the unenlightened. Because the text is not preserved as a continuous bhāṣya, scholars use it cautiously — as supplementary evidence for Bhāskara's reading-strategy on disputed verses (notably 2.12, 7.4–7, 13.2) rather than as an independent doctrinal source. The fragmentary state itself is significant for the history of pre-Rāmānuja Vaiṣṇava Vedānta.
